In this episode

Agent systems require safety evaluation at execution rather than just output level, with code-based harnesses providing the infrastructure for governance, runtime adjudication, and multi-agent coordination—yet current architectures struggle with intermediate-state violations and protocol-level security threats.

Share on LinkedIn tag the author when you post
Copied!
research relevance 0.92

Paper page - Auditing Agent Harness Safety

huggingface.co· Unknown author · 2026-05-18T23:49:16

This research paper introduces HarnessAudit, a framework for evaluating the safety of LLM agent execution harnesses through trajectory-level auditing. The key insight is that agents can produce correct outputs while violating safety constraints during execution—a failure mode invisible to output-level evaluation. The authors created HarnessAudit-Bench, a 210-task benchmark across eight domains, and evaluated ten harness configurations. They found that task completion often misaligns with safe execution, violations accumulate with trajectory length, and multi-agent systems significantly expand the safety risk surface. This work addresses a critical gap in AI safety evaluation by focusing on intermediate execution states rather than terminal outputs.

Key takeaways
  • Output-level safety evaluation misses mid-trajectory violations; full trajectory auditing is necessary for agent harness safety.
  • Task completion and safe execution are misaligned; violations accumulate as trajectory length increases.
  • Multi-agent collaboration expands safety risks; harness design fundamentally bounds safe deployment capability.
  • Resource access and inter-agent information transfer are primary violation points across domains.
  • HarnessAudit-Bench provides systematic evaluation across eight domains with embedded safety constraints for benchmarking.
#LLM agents#multi-agent systems#safety evaluation
Read article →
research relevance 0.92

Code as Agent Harness

arxiv.org· Unknown author · 2026-06-12 (discovered)

This arXiv paper presents a comprehensive survey on "Code as Agent Harness," proposing that code serves as the foundational infrastructure for agentic AI systems beyond mere output generation. The authors organize their analysis across three layers: the harness interface connecting agents to reasoning and action, harness mechanisms including planning, memory, and tool use, and scaling considerations for multi-agent systems. The survey covers applications spanning coding assistants, GUI automation, embodied agents, scientific discovery, and enterprise workflows, while identifying critical open challenges such as evaluation metrics, verification under incomplete feedback, and human oversight for safety-critical operations. This work provides a unified framework for understanding how executable, verifiable, and stateful code infrastructure enables more reliable and capable AI agent systems.

Key takeaways
  • Code functions as operational substrate for agent reasoning, action, environment modeling, and execution-based verification
  • Three-layer architecture: harness interface, mechanisms (planning/memory/tool-use), and multi-agent coordination
  • Spans applications from coding assistants to scientific discovery, GUI automation, and enterprise DevOps workflows
  • Open challenges include evaluation beyond task success, regression-free improvement, and human oversight for safety-critical actions
  • Framework enables executable, verifiable, stateful AI systems with shared code artifacts across multi-agent settings
#AI agents#code generation#agentic systems
Read article →
research relevance 0.92

A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents

arxiv.org· Tallam, Krti · 2026-06-12 (discovered)

This paper presents a five-plane reference architecture for runtime governance of production AI agents, addressing a fundamental shift in enterprise security. Traditional security models protect data boundaries through access control and perimeter inspection, but autonomous agents dissolve this assumption by executing sequences of individually-permitted actions that may transform business processes without explicit authorization. The authors propose a decomposition into a reasoning plane (intent adjudication) and four enforcement planes (network, identity, endpoint, data), combined with composite principals, capability attenuation, and structured audit. The work defines interruption primitives, correctness invariants, and demonstrates threat foreclosure across five workflows, with a reference implementation showing microsecond-scale adjudication and tamper-evident auditing.

Key takeaways
  • Five-plane architecture separates intent reasoning from enforcement across network, identity, endpoint, and data planes for agent governance.
  • Composite principals with capability attenuation handle delegated authority chains, replacing atomic principal-based policy engines.
  • Six interruption primitives generalize allow/deny semantics for stateful, workflow-aware policy decisions in agentic systems.
  • Audit substrate provides structured, tamper-evident evidence of governance decisions at microsecond adjudication latency.
  • Scope limited to delegated action governance, not model behavior—full-system evaluation on live benchmarks identified as future work.
#AI agents#runtime governance#security architecture
Read article →
blog relevance 0.82

Subagents

simonwillison.net· Unknown author · 2026-06-12 (discovered)

This article introduces subagents as a key architectural pattern in agentic systems, where complex tasks are decomposed into specialized agent instances that operate autonomously within defined scopes. Subagents enable modularity, improved fault isolation, and scalability by allowing each agent to focus on a specific domain or capability rather than requiring a monolithic agent to handle all responsibilities. Technical teams should care because this pattern directly addresses challenges in building reliable, maintainable multi-agent systems while reducing cognitive load on individual agents and improving overall system resilience.

Key takeaways
  • Subagents decompose complex tasks into specialized, autonomous agent instances with bounded responsibilities
  • Each subagent operates independently within defined scope, enabling better fault isolation and debugging
  • Pattern improves scalability by distributing work across focused agents rather than centralizing logic
  • Subagents enhance modularity, making systems easier to maintain, test, and extend with new capabilities
  • Clear interface boundaries between subagents reduce complexity and improve system comprehensibility
#agentic systems#design patterns#multi-agent architecture
Read article →
research relevance 0.78

GloriaaaM/LLM-Agent-Harness-Survey · Datasets at Hugging Face

huggingface.co· Unknown author · 2026-04-17T00:00:00

This dataset or survey catalog presents a comparative security threat modeling analysis across four emerging AI-agent communication protocols: MCP (Model Context Protocol), A2A, Agora, and ANP. The work appears to focus on identifying vulnerabilities and security considerations unique to agent-to-agent and agent-to-system communication paradigms. For technical researchers building or deploying multi-agent systems, understanding threat landscapes across these competing protocol standards is critical for selecting secure architectures and implementing appropriate mitigations before production deployment.

Key takeaways
  • Four major AI-agent protocols analyzed for security vulnerabilities: MCP, A2A, Agora, ANP
  • Threat modeling reveals protocol-specific attack surfaces in agent communication
  • Security analysis essential for teams selecting or implementing agent frameworks
  • Comparative approach enables informed protocol selection based on threat tolerance
#AI agents#security threat modeling#protocols
Read article →
blog relevance 0.78

https://community.openai.com/t/open-source-cybertron-a-governance-first-architecture-for-agentic-ai-systems/1370412

community.openai.com· Unknown author · 2026-06-12 (discovered)

This post introduces Cybertron, an open-source governance-first architecture designed for agentic AI systems. The core thesis is that as AI agents become more autonomous and powerful, they require robust governance frameworks embedded at the architectural level rather than bolted on afterward. Cybertron prioritizes safety constraints, policy enforcement, and auditability as first-class design concerns, enabling organizations to deploy agents with confidence. The architecture is relevant to teams building production AI systems at scale, particularly those facing regulatory requirements or managing high-stakes autonomous workflows.

Key takeaways
  • Governance-first architecture embeds safety policies and constraints at the core design level, not as afterthoughts
  • Cybertron provides auditability and policy enforcement mechanisms essential for regulated agentic AI deployments
  • Open-source design allows community scrutiny and collaborative hardening of critical safety components
  • Architecture enables teams to maintain human oversight and control over increasingly autonomous agent behaviors
#agentic AI#governance#safety architectures
Read article →
blog relevance 0.78

Coding Agents

simonwillison.net· Unknown author · 2026-06-12 (discovered)

This article examines coding agents—AI systems that autonomously write, debug, and optimize code. The piece explores design patterns and architectural decisions for building effective code-generating agents, covering how they integrate with development workflows, handle context windows, and maintain code quality. For technical teams, understanding these patterns is critical as AI-assisted development becomes mainstream, offering insights into agent design tradeoffs and integration strategies that impact developer productivity and code reliability.

Key takeaways
  • Coding agents require careful context management to handle large codebases within token limits
  • Design patterns should balance autonomy with human oversight for safety and debugging
  • Effective agents combine code generation with testing and validation loops
  • Integration with version control and IDE workflows improves practical utility
  • Agent performance depends on prompt engineering and retrieval of relevant code context
#AI agents#code generation#software engineering
